{{short description|Public demonstration of corporate support or opposition to a partisan sociopolitical issue}}
{{use mdy dates |date=November 2021}}
{{climate change and society|Economics/Finance}}

'''Corporate sociopolitical activism''' ('''CSA''') refers to a firm's public demonstration of support or opposition to a partisan sociopolitical issue.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Lin |first=Tom C.W. |title=The Capitalist and the Activist |publisher=Berrett-Koehler Publishers |year=2022 |isbn=978-1-5230-9255-0 |pages=21–23 |oclc=1285525992}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Bhagwat|first1=Yashoda|last2=Warren|first2=Nooshin L.|last3=Beck|first3=Joshua T.|last4=George F. Watson|first4=I. V.|date=2020-06-29|title=Corporate Sociopolitical Activism and Firm Value|url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0022242920937000|journal=Journal of Marketing|volume=84|issue=5|pages=1–21|language=en|doi=10.1177/0022242920937000|hdl=10150/643357|s2cid=220987121|hdl-access=free|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=Mukherjee|first1=Sourjo|last2=Althuizen|first2=Niek|date=2020-03-13|title=Brand activism: Does courting controversy help or hurt a brand?|journal=International Journal of Research in Marketing|volume=37|issue=4|pages=772–788|language=en|doi=10.1016/j.ijresmar.2020.02.008|s2cid=216296266 |issn=0167-8116|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last=Moorman|first=Christine|date=2020-09-15|title=Commentary: Brand Activism in a Political World|url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0743915620945260|journal=Journal of Public Policy & Marketing|volume=39|issue=4|pages=388–392|language=en|doi=10.1177/0743915620945260|s2cid=221749372|url-access=subscription}}</ref> CSA has become increasingly prominent in the 2010s and 2020s, as firms have taken stances on issues such as [[climate change]], [[racial justice]], [[reproductive rights]], [[gun control]], immigration, [[LGBTQ rights]], and [[gender equality]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Kotler|first1=Philip|last2=Sarkar|first2=Christian |title=Finally, Brand Activism! |url=https://www.marketingjournal.org/finally-brand-activism-philip-kotler-and-christian-sarkar/ |website=The Marketing Journal |date=January 9, 2017 |publisher=Double Loop Marketing LLC |access-date=2020-11-19}}</ref>

Several terms, such as '''woke capitalism''', '''woke capital''', and '''stakeholder capitalism''',<ref name="hutton">{{cite news |last=Hutton |first=Will |title='Woke' capitalism is the new villain of the right. It's also the only way forward |date=30 January 2022 |website=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2022/jan/30/woke-capitalism-new-villain-of-the-right-only-way-forward }}</ref> are used by some commentators to refer to a form of marketing, advertising, and [[corporate structure]]s that pertains to sociopolitical standpoints tied to [[social justice]] and [[activist]] causes.<ref name="Bartleby Jan 2019">{{cite news |author=<!--anonymous author, no byline--> |title=Woke, not broke |department=Bartleby |url=https://www.economist.com/business/2019/01/26/companies-can-appeal-to-workers-and-consumers-with-liberal-messages |url-access=limited |newspaper=[[The Economist]] |issue=9127 |volume=430 |date=26 January 2019 |issn=0013-0613 |page=65}}</ref> The term was coined by columnist [[Ross Douthat]] in "The Rise of Woke Capital", an article written for and published in ''[[The New York Times]]'' in 2018.{{r|Lewis 2020}}<ref name="Douthat 2018">{{cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/02/28/opinion/corporate-america-activism.html |title=The Rise of Woke Capital |website=[[The New York Times]] |first=Ross |last=Douthat |date=February 28, 2018}}</ref>

CSA is comparable but distinct from two related firm activities: [[corporate social responsibility]] (CSR) and corporate political activity (CPA). Firms may engage in CSA to appeal to purpose-driven ideals, as well as contribute to more strategic motives, in line with consumers' existing preferences for moral purchasing options. A recent study found that 64% of global consumers choose to buy or boycott a given brand on the basis of its political leanings, a result suggesting the increasing importance of [[ethical consumerism]] practices.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|title=Two-Thirds of Consumers Worldwide Now Buy on Beliefs|url=https://www.edelman.com/news-awards/two-thirds-consumers-worldwide-now-buy-beliefs|access-date=2020-11-18|website=Edelman|date=October 2, 2018 |language=en}}</ref>

A 2020 survey of [[Chief marketing officer|CMOs]] revealed that a growing proportion of marketing leaders find it acceptable to make changes to products and services in response to political issues (47%), have executives speak out on political issues (33%), and use marketing communications to speak out on political issues (28%).<ref name=":2" /> Furthermore, brands engaged in an unprecedented level of activist behavior in response to consumers protesting racial injustice in 2020.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2020-07-07|title=How brands and agencies responded to racial injustice in the first month following George Floyd's death|url=https://adage.com/article/cmo-strategy/how-brands-and-agencies-responded-racial-injustice-first-month-following-george-floyds-death/2265626|access-date=2020-11-18|website=Adage.com|language=en}}</ref>

Firms have historically strayed from vocalizing stances on controversial sociopolitical matters, with the understanding that doing so could sever certain [[Stakeholder (corporate)|stakeholder]] relationships;<ref name=":0" /> however, modern cultural shifts have precipitated a "hyper-partisan" climate,<ref>{{Cite journal|author1-link=James N. Druckman|last1=Druckman|first1=James N.|last2=Peterson|first2=Erik|last3=Slothuus|first3=Rune|date=2013|title=How Elite Partisan Polarization Affects Public Opinion Formation|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23357757|journal=The American Political Science Review|volume=107|issue=1|pages=57–79|doi=10.1017/S0003055412000500|jstor=23357757|s2cid=8282836|issn=0003-0554}}</ref> leading to demand for firms to exercise purpose-driven efforts in the marketplace.<ref>{{Cite web|last=O'Hara|first=Mary Emily|title=Consumers Want Brands to Hire More People of Color|url=https://www.adweek.com/brand-marketing/black-lives-matter-consumers-expect-diversity-hiring/|website=adage.com|date=2020-07-15|access-date=2020-11-18|language=en-US}}</ref> As stated by [[Richard Edelman]], chief executive officer (CEO) of [[Edelman (firm)|Edelman]], "Brands are now being pushed to go beyond their classic business interests to become advocates. It is a new relationship between a company and consumer, where a purchase is premised on the brand's willingness to live its values, act with purpose, and, if necessary, make the leap into activism."<ref name=":3" />

== Definition ==
CSA is a unique form of cause-related [[Company|firm]] behavior defined broadly by two distinct characteristics: publicity and partisanship. Specifically, CSA involves a firm's ''public'' support of or opposition to a ''partisan'' sociopolitical issue. Such issues are described as "salient unresolved social matters on which societal and institutional opinion is split, thus potentially engendering acrimonious debate among groups".<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Nalick|first1=Michael|last2=Josefy|first2=Matthew|last3=Zardkoohi|first3=Asghar|last4=Bierman|first4=Leonard|date=2016-10-06|title=Corporate Sociopolitical Involvement: A Reflection of Whose Preferences?|url=https://journals.aom.org/doi/10.5465/amp.2015.0033|journal=Academy of Management Perspectives|volume=30|issue=4|pages=384–403|doi=10.5465/amp.2015.0033|issn=1558-9080|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Notably, while the controversy surrounding a given issue can change or be resolved, a firm's efforts may be considered CSA to the extent that they reflect engagement with an issue defined as partisan at a given point in time, politics, and culture. Furthermore, the term "brand activism" has been used to describe similar efforts by individual brands (i.e. owned by firms) to vocalize public stances on sociopolitical issues; brand activism can, thus, be considered CSA delivered through a brand's voice.{{citation needed|date=January 2025}}

=== Conceptual distinctions ===

==== Corporate social responsibility ====
{{Main|Corporate social responsibility}}
[[File:Distinctions CSA.png|thumb|Conceptual distinctions among CSA, CSR, and CPA]]

CSR involves firms' contributions to widely favored societal goals (e.g. community resources, education, and donations to disease prevention research) via philanthropic or charitable efforts,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kang|first1=Charles|last2=Germann|first2=Frank|last3=Grewal|first3=Rajdeep|date=2016-03-01|title=Washing Away Your Sins? Corporate Social Responsibility, Corporate Social Irresponsibility, and Firm Performance|url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1509/jm.15.0324|journal=Journal of Marketing|volume=80|issue=2|pages=59–79|language=en|doi=10.1509/jm.15.0324|s2cid=167797490|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Varadarajan|first1=P. Rajan|last2=Menon|first2=Anil|date=2018-11-19|title=Cause-Related Marketing: A Coalignment of Marketing Strategy and Corporate Philanthropy|url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/002224298805200306|journal=Journal of Marketing|volume=52|issue=3|pages=58–74|language=en|doi=10.1177/002224298805200306|s2cid=53683924|url-access=subscription}}</ref> CSA pertains to a firm's engagement in causes for which there is no universally acceptable correct response.<ref name=":0" /> Furthermore, CSA may involve a lower level of monetary investment (e.g. a press release or an open letter) compared to CSR; however, there is greater risk associated with CSA, due in part to the potential for backlash from various stakeholders.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" />

==== Corporate political activity ====
CSA is distinct from CPA—a firm's efforts (e.g. campaign contributions, [[lobbying]], and donations to [[political action committee]]s) to sway political processes and gain policy-related market advantages.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Lux|first1=Sean|last2=Crook|first2=T. Russell|last3=Woehr|first3=David J.|date=2010-12-20|title=Mixing Business With Politics: A Meta-Analysis of the Antecedents and Outcomes of Corporate Political Activity|url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0149206310392233|journal=Journal of Management|volume=37|pages=223–247|language=en|doi=10.1177/0149206310392233|s2cid=144560276|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Hillman|first1=Amy J.|last2=Keim|first2=Gerald D.|last3=Schuler|first3=Douglas|date=2016-06-23|title=Corporate Political Activity: A Review and Research Agenda|url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1016/j.jm.2004.06.003|journal=Journal of Management|volume=30|issue=6|pages=837–857|language=en|doi=10.1016/j.jm.2004.06.003|s2cid=153503017|url-access=subscription}}</ref> While both types of firm activities reflect involvement in the political process, they differ in the extent to which they are publicized. CSA is often utilized as a public demonstration of a firm's core values and principles. Conversely, CPA is an often-discreet activity that is typically made public only through "accidental disclosure".<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Werner|first=Timothy|date=2017|title=Investor Reaction to Covert Corporate Political Activity|url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/smj.2682|journal=Strategic Management Journal|language=en|volume=38|issue=12|pages=2424–2443|doi=10.1002/smj.2682|issn=1097-0266|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

== Examples ==
[[File:Times Rugrats Was Really Progressive! - NickRewind.webm|thumb|2019 promotional video from [[Nickelodeon]] presenting various instances of progressive messaging in the 1990s series ''[[Rugrats]]'']]
Firms have increasingly taken activist stances on sociopolitical issues across a variety of domains.

=== Racial justice ===
Firms have spoken out about racial justice in a number of ways (e.g., affirming support for the [[Black Lives Matter]] movement and donating a portion of profits to [[Civil and political rights|civil rights]] organizations). Among the most prominent examples of racial justice CSAs came in September 2018, when [[Nike, Inc.|Nike]] announced football player [[Colin Kaepernick]] as the spokesperson for its thirtieth-anniversary advertising campaign.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Draper|first1=Kevin|last2=Belson|first2=Ken|date=2018-09-03|title=Colin Kaepernick's Nike Campaign Keeps N.F.L. Anthem Kneeling in Spotlight (Published 2018)|language=en-US|work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/09/03/sports/kaepernick-nike.html|access-date=2020-11-18|issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Notably, Kaepernick stirred national debate in 2016 by kneeling during the National Anthem in protest of [[Racial inequality in the United States|racial inequality]] and [[Police brutality in the United States|police brutality]] in the United States.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Witz|first=Billy|date=2016-09-02|title=This Time, Colin Kaepernick Takes a Stand by Kneeling (Published 2016)|language=en-US|work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2016/09/02/sports/football/colin-kaepernick-kneels-national-anthem-protest.html|access-date=2020-11-18|issn=0362-4331}}</ref> In Nike's campaign, Kaepernick said, "Believe in something, even if it means sacrificing everything." News agencies characterized this tagline as implicit support for Kaepernick's platform of racial justice advocacy.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Branch|first=John|date=2017-09-07|title=The Awakening of Colin Kaepernick (Published 2017)|language=en-US|work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/09/07/sports/colin-kaepernick-nfl-protests.html|access-date=2020-11-18|issn=0362-4331}}</ref>

While Nike's decision initially sparked consumer backlash and a dip in its stock price, the firm's value reached an all-time high only a week later.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Nike's Big Bet on Colin Kaepernick Campaign Continues to Pay Off |url=https://fortune.com/2018/12/21/nike-stock-colin-kaepernick/ |date=2018-12-21 |access-date=2020-11-18 |website=Fortune |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Youn |first=Soo |title=Nike sales booming after Colin Kaepernick ad, invalidating critics |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Business/nike-sales-booming-kaepernick-ad-invalidating-critics/story?id=59957137 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181222022443/https://abcnews.go.com/Business/nike-sales-booming-kaepernick-ad-invalidating-critics/story?id=59957137 |archive-date=December 22, 2018 |date=2018-12-21 |access-date=2020-11-18 |website=ABC}}</ref> According to a Quinnipiac University poll, much of the persistent consumer support for the ad came from consumers between 18 and 34, two-thirds of whom approved of Nike's actions.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Boren |first=Cindy |title=Nike's Colin Kaepernick ad campaign gets more yeas than nays from young people |language=en-US |newspaper=Washington Post |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/early-lead/wp/2018/09/13/colin-kaepernicks-nike-ad-campaign-gets-more-yeahs-than-nays-from-young-people/ |access-date=2020-11-18 |issn=0190-8286}}</ref> This case has been considered a critical turning point in the emergence of CSA as a prominent brand practice. Additional examples of racial justice CSAs include the following:
* [[The Home Depot|Home Depot]] CEO Craig Meaner said in a statement, "We are all confronting deep pain and anguish over the senseless killing of [[George Floyd]], [[Murder of Ahmaud Arbery|Ahmaud Arbery]], and other unarmed Black men and women in our country. We cannot ignore that their deaths are part of a pattern of racism and reflect the harsh reality that as a nation we are much too far from fulfilling the promise of equal justice for all."<ref>{{Cite web|date=2020-06-01|title=Message from Craig Menear – Racial Equality & Justice for All|url=https://corporate.homedepot.com/newsroom/message-craig-menear-%E2%80%93-racial-equality-justice-all|access-date=2020-11-18|website=The Home Depot|language=en}}</ref>
* [[Netflix]] promoted a new Black Lives Matter collection to U.S. subscribers, featuring a number of television and movie titles about racial injustice and the experience of Black Americans.<ref>{{Cite web|last1=Spangler|first1=Todd|date=2020-06-10|title=Netflix Launches 'Black Lives Matter' Collection of Movies, TV Shows and Documentaries|url=https://variety.com/2020/digital/news/netflix-black-lives-matter-collection-1234630160/|access-date=2020-11-18|website=Variety|language=en-US}}</ref>
* [[Walmart]] announced that it will donate $100 million over five years to create a new center for racial equity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Making a Difference in Racial Equity: Walmart CEO Doug McMillon's Full Remarks|url=https://corporate.walmart.com/equity|access-date=2020-11-18|website=Corporate - US|language=en-US|archive-date=November 17, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201117202913/https://corporate.walmart.com/equity|url-status=dead}}</ref>

=== LGBTQ rights ===
[[File:Pride Parade 2019 (48458163557).jpg|thumb|A [[Bud Light]] advertisement at an [[LGBTQ pride]] event]]

Firms have utilized both internal and external resources to take a stand on issues facing the [[LGBTQ]] community. For instance, many firms act as corporate sponsors of [[Pride parade]]s internationally and assist in LGBTQ community-building efforts.<ref>{{Cite news|editor=Drew Goins, Alyssa Rosenberg|title=Opinion- As more companies wade in, it's time to ask: Is Pride for sale?|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/graphics/2019/opinions/pride-for-sale/|date=2019-06-20|access-date=2020-11-18|newspaper=Washington Post|language=en}}</ref> Published since 2002, the [[Human Rights Campaign]] has utilized its [[Corporate Equality Index]] (CEI) to measure the extent to which American businesses treat equitably their LGBTQ employees, customers, and investors. Criteria used to assess companies include, among others, a written policy of non-discrimination based on sexual orientation, gender identity, and gender expression; appropriate and respectful advertising to the LGBTQ community; and transgender-inclusive health benefits.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Corporate Equality Index|url=https://www.hrc.org/resources/corporate-equality-index|access-date=2020-11-18|website=HRC|language=en-US}}</ref>

There has been an increase in the number of firms with a perfect CEI rating every year since the tool's inception. Such firms frequently release statements and press releases to express satisfaction at having been recognized for their diversity and inclusivity efforts. Notably, many brands have also released Pride-related merchandise in recent years to signal support for LGBTQ rights, as well as position themselves as advocates for LGBTQ consumers.
* [[Converse (shoe company)|Converse]] released a collection of Pride-inspired low- and high-top sneakers featuring a rainbow flag with a brown and black stripe dedicated to queer people of color. Also included in this collection was a pair of sneakers adorned with the pink, light blue, and white colors of the [[transgender]] flag.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2020-05-27|title=Converse and Nike Just Revealed Their Pride 2020 Shoes|url=https://www.out.com/pride/2020/5/27/converse-and-nike-just-revealed-their-pride-2020-shoes|access-date=2020-11-18|website=out.com|language=en}}</ref>
* [[Sephora]]'s "We Love Pride" make-up collection featured a metallic red lipstick called "Love Is Love".<ref>{{Cite web|last=Prinzivalli|first=Leah|title=Sephora Wants to Celebrate Pride With You|url=https://www.allure.com/story/sephora-pride-2019-identify-as-we-campaign|access-date=2020-11-18|website=Allure|date=June 4, 2019 |language=en-us}}</ref> The brand donated a portion of sales to a variety of LGBTQ charities.
* [[Fossil Group|Fossil]] released its second annual Pride Watch, featuring a bezel with all the colors of the rainbow flag. U.S. sales benefitted the [[Hetrick-Martin Institute]], the nation's oldest and largest LGBTQ youth organization.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Fossil: Make Time For Good|url=https://hmi.org/fossil/|access-date=2020-11-18|website=Hetrick-Martin Institute|language=en-US}}</ref>

=== Climate change ===
While [[sustainable business]] practices have long been a component of firms' CSR activities, some companies have taken an activist stance in recent years to [[climate change mitigation|address climate change]]  more explicitly. For example, the brand [[Patagonia, Inc.|Patagonia]] has established itself as a chief market-based [[environmental justice]] advocate. Its November 2011 "Don't Buy This Jacket" spot in the ''[[The New York Times|New York Times]]'' served as both an advertisement for the firm's merchandise and an imperative for consumers to reduce their carbon footprint.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2011-11-25|title=Don't Buy This Jacket, Black Friday and the New York Times - Patagonia|url=https://www.patagonia.com/stories/dont-buy-this-jacket-black-friday-and-the-new-york-times/story-18615.html|access-date=2020-11-18|website=patagonia.com|language=en}}</ref> The ad's message leveraged an [[anti-consumerist]] ideology to encourage the purchase of long-lasting outdoor apparel and deter the proliferation of the [[fast fashion]] industry.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Farré|first=Kenji|date=2020-06-11|title="Don't Buy This Jacket" — Patagonia's Daring Campaign|url=https://medium.com/better-marketing/dont-buy-this-jacket-patagonia-s-daring-campaign-2b37e145046b|access-date=2020-11-18|website=Medium|language=en}}</ref> Most large companies across the world have made commitments to climate change.<ref>{{Cite web |title=CDP A List companies 2023 |url=https://www.cdp.net/en/companies/companies-scores |access-date=2024-12-23 |website=www.cdp.net |language=en}}</ref> For example:
* [[Amazon (company)|Amazon]] announced in 2019 that it would transition to 80% renewable energy usage by 2024, and then to zero emissions by 2030.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Amazon Sustainability|url=https://sustainability.aboutamazon.com/|access-date=2020-11-18|website=Amazon|language=en}}</ref> The firm's CEO [[Jeff Bezos]] also launched the Bezos Earth Fund in February 2020, committing $10 billion to assist in "any effort that offers a real possibility to help preserve and protect the natural world".<ref>{{Cite web|last=Calma|first=Justine|date=2020-02-19|title=How climate scientists, activists, and NGOs want to spend Jeff Bezos' money|url=https://www.theverge.com/2020/2/19/21143044/jeff-bezos-climate-change-donation-scientists-earth-fund-activists-ngo|access-date=2020-11-18|website=The Verge|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=Palmer|first=Annie|date=2020-11-16|title=Jeff Bezos names first recipients of his $10 billion Earth Fund for combating climate change|url=https://www.cnbc.com/2020/11/16/jeff-bezos-names-first-recipients-of-his-10-billion-earth-fund.html|access-date=2020-11-18|website=CNBC|language=en}}</ref>
* [[Google]] announced in September 2020 that it is investing in manufacturing regions to create new carbon-free energy and help cities reduce their emissions.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2020-09-14|title=Our third decade of climate action: Realizing a carbon-free future|url=https://blog.google/outreach-initiatives/sustainability/our-third-decade-climate-action-realizing-carbon-free-future/|access-date=2020-11-18|website=Google|language=en}}</ref>

=== Gun control ===
A number of major firearms sellers have modified their gun sales policies, particularly as a response to [[Mass shootings in the United States|mass shootings]] taking place in the U.S. Two such notable examples are the following:
* Ed Stack, CEO of [[Dick's Sporting Goods]], announced in February 2018 that stores would end the sales of [[High-capacity magazine ban|high-capacity magazines]], as well as sales of guns to persons under the age of 21. The firm also took legal action by urging Congress to ban [[Assault weapon|assault-style weapons]], raise the minimum age to purchase a gun to 21, and outlaw sales of high-capacity magazines and [[bump stock]]s.<ref>{{Cite magazine|last=Shephard|first=Alex|date=2018-02-28|title=Dick's Sporting Goods, which sold a gun to the Parkland shooter in November, will no longer sell assault rifles.|magazine=The New Republic|url=https://newrepublic.com/article/147232/dicks-sporting-goods-sold-gun-parkland-shooter-november-will-no-longer-sell-assault-rifles|access-date=2020-11-18|issn=0028-6583}}</ref> The firm cited the [[Parkland high school shooting]] in Parkland, Florida, as an event that directly influenced its decisions.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Soul-Searching After Parkland, Dick's CEO Embraces Tougher Stance On Guns|url=https://www.npr.org/2019/02/12/691999347/soul-searching-after-parkland-dicks-ceo-embraces-tougher-stance-on-guns|access-date=2020-11-18|website=NPR.org|language=en}}</ref>
* Walmart CEO [[Doug McMillon]] released a statement in 2019 containing news of the firm's plans to discontinue sales of short-barrel rifle ammunition, handgun ammunition, and handguns. This statement also requested that customers no longer openly carry firearms into Walmart or Sam's Club stores, including those in states in which [[Open carry in the United States|"open carry"]] is permitted. The firm cited the [[2019 El Paso Walmart shooting]], which took place in a Walmart store, as a critical incident shaping its decisions.<ref>{{Cite web|title=McMillon to Associates: Our Next Steps in Response to the Tragedies in El Paso and Southaven|url=https://corporate.walmart.com/newsroom/2019/09/03/mcmillon-to-associates-our-next-steps-in-response-to-the-tragedies-in-el-paso-and-southaven|access-date=2020-11-18|website=Walmart.com|language=en-US}}</ref>

=== Other domains ===
Firms have engaged in CSA in a number of other domains. Below are select examples.

==== Reproductive health care ====
[[MAC Cosmetics|M.A.C. Cosmetics]] has worked with [[Planned Parenthood]] since 2008 and contributed over $2 million to the organization.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Benefit Cosmetics and M∙A∙C Cosmetics Announce Support for Planned Parenthood|url=https://www.plannedparenthood.org/about-us/newsroom/press-releases/benefit-cosmetics-and-m-a-c-cosmetics-announce-support-for-planned-parenthood|access-date=2020-11-18|website=Planned Parenthood|language=en}}</ref> According to John Demsey, executive group president of the brand's parent company [[Estée Lauder Companies|Estée Lauder]], "It is so important for people of all ages, all races and all genders to get the accurate information and care they need so they can live their best, healthiest lives, but we see that a lot of people aren't seeking that information and care because of stigmas that disproportionately affect women, people of color and the LGBTQ community."<ref>{{Cite web|last=Shatzman|first=Celia|title=MAC And Benefit Announce Support For Planned Parenthood|url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/celiashatzman/2019/03/12/mac-and-benefit-announce-support-for-planned-parenthood/|access-date=2020-11-18|website=Forbes|language=en}}</ref>

==== Net neutrality ====
[[Burger King]] advocated for [[net neutrality]] with a January 2018 ad that illustrated the concept of paid prioritization through hamburger sales—customers were told they would have to wait longer for their food, unless they were willing to pay a premium for immediate service.<ref>{{Cite magazine|first=Katie|last=Reilly|date=2018-01-24|title=Allow Burger King's New Ad to Explain Net Neutrality to You|url=https://time.com/5117628/burger-king-whopper-net-neutrality-ad/|access-date=2020-11-18|magazine=Time}}</ref>

==== Gender non-discrimination ====
Target issued a statement in September 2016 encouraging store employees and patrons to use the restroom or fitting room facility that corresponds with their [[gender identity]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Continuing to Stand for Inclusivity|url=http://corporate.target.com/article/2016/04/target-stands-inclusivity|access-date=2020-11-18|website=Target|language=en}}</ref>

==== Immigration ====
In January 2017, nearly 100 Silicon Valley firms filed an [[Amicus curiae|amicus brief]] against the Trump administration's [[Executive Order 13769|anti-immigration policy]] directed at refugees, travelers, and visa holders originating from predominantly Muslim portions of the world.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Chandler|first=Adam|date=2017-02-06|title=Silicon Valley's Biggest Companies Denounce the Immigration Ban in Court|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/business/archive/2017/02/silicon-valley-immigration-ban/515802/|access-date=2020-11-18|website=The Atlantic|language=en-US}}</ref>

==== Controversial symbols ====
[[NASCAR]] announced in July 2020 that it would ban the [[Confederate flag]] from all its racing venues.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Almasy|first=Steve|date=2020-06-10|title=NASCAR bans Confederate flags at all races, events|url=https://www.cnn.com/2020/06/10/us/nascar-bans-confederate-flag-spt-trnd/index.html|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200610225607/https://www.cnn.com/2020/06/10/us/nascar-bans-confederate-flag-spt-trnd/index.html |archive-date=June 10, 2020 |access-date=2020-11-18|website=CNN}}</ref>

== Potential business benefits ==
Research has uncovered the broader financial implications of CSA on firm value including improving firm's attractiveness to a wider segment of investors and customers. On average, investors respond negatively to CSA, though there are a number of factors that may buffer or even reverse this relationship. Most notably, a firm's CSA elicits positive abnormal stock returns when there is high alignment between the firm's CSA and the values of its stakeholders (e.g. customers, employees, and state legislators). In particular, researchers observed an increase in sales growth over the next quarter and year when CSA aligned with customer values.<ref name=":0" />

A number of CSA characteristics have been shown to further heighten investor response: if the activism takes the form of an action, is announced by the CEO, is not justified by a business objective, and is announced alone (vs. in a coalition with other firms). Notably, managers may find it especially appropriate to engage in CSA if they are deeply committed to activism, and it aligns with their strategic objectives (i.e. acquiring a more liberal or conservative customer base).<ref name=":0" /> Still, CSA requires strategic deliberation. CSA activities may signal to stakeholders that the firm is willing to engage in risky behaviors and even divert resources from profit-generating activities. Given the enduring nature of activism, it is often plausible for investors to believe CSA serves as a value-based indication of a firm's future decisions, particularly those related to purpose, reputation, and relationship management.<ref name=":0" />

== Criticism and concerns ==
Critics have expressed concern about the degree to which CSA is helpful, either for advancing sociopolitical causes or as a firm activity more generally.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last=Lin |first=Tom C.W. |date=2018 |title=Incorporating Social Activism |journal=Boston University Law Review |volume=98 |pages=1573–1593 |via=Hein Online}}</ref> By the mid-2010s, forms of rhetoric that were later retroactively labelled as "[[woke]]" had entered mainstream media and were being used in marketing and advertising;<ref name="Sobande">{{cite journal |first=Francesca |last=Sobande |title=Woke-washing: 'Intersectional' femvertising and branding 'woke' bravery |year=2019 |journal=European Journal of Marketing |volume=54 |issue=11 |pages=2723–2745 |issn=0309-0566 |url=http://orca.cf.ac.uk/126357/16/Woke-washing%20-%20%27Intersectional%27%20femvertising%20and%20branding%20%27woke%27%20bravery%20%28Francesca%20Sobande%29%20-%20PDF.pdf |doi=10.1108/EJM-02-2019-0134 |s2cid=213469381 |quote=adverts span from 2015–2018, which reflects the point at which the language of 'woke(ness)' entered mainstream media and marketing spheres |access-date=January 5, 2024 |archive-date=November 13, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201113153558/http://orca.cf.ac.uk/126357/16/Woke-washing%20-%20%27Intersectional%27%20femvertising%20and%20branding%20%27woke%27%20bravery%20%28Francesca%20Sobande%29%20-%20PDF.pdf |url-status=dead }}</ref> campaigns associated with this trend have been generally perceived by consumers as insincere and inauthentic, and have provoked cultural [[Backlash (sociology)|backlash]]es.<ref name="Mirzaei">{{Cite web |url=http://theconversation.com/where-woke-came-from-and-why-marketers-should-think-twice-before-jumping-on-the-social-activism-bandwagon-122713 |title=Where 'woke' came from and why marketers should think twice before jumping on the social activism bandwagon |first=Abas |last=Mirzaei |website=The Conversation |date=September 8, 2019 |accessdate=July 11, 2021}}</ref>

Cultural scientists Akane Kanai and [[Rosalind Gill]] described woke capitalism as a then-"dramatically intensifying" trend in which [[public relations]] pertains to the concerns of historically marginalized groups (such as in terms of race, gender and religion), using them as [[mascot]]s in advertisements with messages of [[empowerment]]. On the one hand, this creates an individualized and depoliticized idea of social justice, using depictions of social action to signify an increase in self-confidence; on the other hand, the omnipresent visibility in advertising of minorities can also amplify a [[Backlash (sociology)|backlash]] against their equality. For people in lower economic strata, the equality of these minorities thus becomes indispensable to the maintenance of capitalism, with the minorities being seen as responsible for the losses of the system.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kanai |first1=A. |last2=Gill |first2=R. |date=2020-10-28 |title=Woke? Affect, neoliberalism, marginalised identities and consumer culture |url=https://openaccess.city.ac.uk/id/eprint/25176/ |journal=New Formations: A Journal of Culture, Theory & Politics |volume=102 |issue=102 |pages=10–27 |doi=10.3898/NewF:102.01.2020 |s2cid=234623282 |language=en |issn=0950-2378}}</ref>

=== Woke-washing ===
The term ''woke-washing'' was used in 2019 by [[Alan Jope]], chief executive of [[Unilever]], who warned that brands which failed to take verifiable action on their rhetoric could "further destroy trust in our industry".<ref>{{cite news |last=Davies |first=Rob |title=Unilever boss says brands using 'woke-washing' destroy trust |website=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/media/2019/jun/19/unilever-boss-says-brands-using-woke-washing-destroy-trust |accessdate=July 5, 2021}}</ref> [[Helen Lewis (journalist)|Helen Lewis]] held the opinion that [[cancel culture]] is the result of what she calls "the iron law of woke capitalism", and believes that it is used for inexpensive messaging as a substitute for genuine reform.<ref name="Lewis 2020">{{cite web |last=Lewis |first=Helen |title=How Capitalism Drives Cancel Culture |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/international/archive/2020/07/cancel-culture-and-problem-woke-capitalism/614086/ |website=The Atlantic |date=14 July 2020 |url-access=limited}}</ref> [[Will Hutton]] wrote that he believed woke capitalism is "the only way forward", citing principles of corporate responsibility.<ref name="hutton" /> Alternatively, [[Elizabeth Bruenig]] observed that while woke capitalism has been seen as an evolution of capitalism that can create unprecedented benefits for the public good, it remains a form of capitalism and hence cannot be celebrated without aligning with capitalist interests;<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2021/04/28/opinion/liberals-corporations-woke-capitalism.html |title=Opinion &#124; The Rise of 'Woke Capital' Is Nothing to Celebrate |first=Elizabeth |last=Bruenig |date=April 28, 2021 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]]}}</ref> similarly, Andrew V. Abela held the opinion that it does little to actually further progressive causes.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/woke-capitalism-in-a-secular-world-11606420629 |title=Opinion &#124; Woke Capitalism in a Secular World |first=Andrew V. |last=Abela |date=November 26, 2020 |newspaper=[[The Wall Street Journal]]}}</ref>

A common argument is that firms are profit-seeking and thus care more about image and reputation than the causes they address. Some have referred to firms' political behavior as akin to "[[woke-washing]]", a pejorative term adapted from the similar concepts of "[[greenwashing]]" and "[[Pinkwashing (LGBTQ)|pinkwashing]]". Woke-washing is a critique leveraged against firms thought to "appropriate the language of social activism into marketing material".<ref name=":4">{{Cite news|first1=Erin|last1=Dowell|first2=Marlette|last2=Jackson|date=2020-07-27|title='Woke-Washing' Your Company Won't Cut It|work=Harvard Business Review|url=https://hbr.org/2020/07/woke-washing-your-company-wont-cut-it|access-date=2020-11-18|issn=0017-8012}}</ref> Critics have further argued that firms may utilize greater capital on the appearance of [[progressivism]] (i.e. through advertisements and promotional efforts) than on actual cause-related awareness or fundraising efforts. In such circumstances, activism has been criticized as a deceptive marketing tool for capturing demand among belief-driven consumers.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Jones|first=Owen|date=2019-05-23|title=Woke-washing: how brands are cashing in on the culture wars|language=en-GB|work=The Guardian|url=https://www.theguardian.com/media/2019/may/23/woke-washing-brands-cashing-in-on-culture-wars-owen-jones|access-date=2020-11-18|issn=0261-3077}}</ref> Action-based follow-through could be important for fostering perceptions of authentic connection to supported sociopolitical causes. In a 2020 ''[[Harvard Business Review]]'' article, journalists Erin Dowell and Marlette Jackson said, "Empty company statements can seem to say that Black lives only matter to big business when there's profit to be made."<ref name=":4" /> Others have argued whether firms should engage with sociopolitical issues at all. In particular, some critics have shunned the idea that market-based entities should influence or have a say in what is considered right and wrong.{{Citation needed|date=February 2024}}

=== Corroding democratic values ===
Some warn that the rise of corporate social activism could corrode democratic values and threaten the public's faith in democratic systems.<ref name=":5" /> The "market values of businesses could supplant the moral values of society" and social activism for the public good may not align with a corporation's driving force: profits.<ref name=":5" />

== <span class="anchor" id="Counter-movement"></span>"Go woke, go broke" counter-movement ==
Beginning to a major degree in the 2020s, members of the American right have perpetuated efforts to boycott companies that openly support "woke" causes. The phrase "Go woke, go broke" has been an [[Politics of the United States|American political]] umbrella [[catchphrase]] used by [[Conservatism in the United States|right-wing]] groups to criticize and boycott businesses publicly supporting [[Progressivism in the United States|progressive]] policies, including [[empowering women]], [[LGBTQ people]] and [[critical race theory]] ("going [[woke]]"), claiming that [[stock value]] and business performance will inevitably suffer ("going [[wiktionary:broke|broke]]") as a result of adopting values of [[diversity, equity, and inclusion]]. Many companies subject to "go woke go broke" campaigns, including [[AB InBev]], [[Target Corporation|Target]], and [[the Walt Disney Company]] have seen declines in revenue, profit, and/or stock value as a result of the "go woke go broke" backlash campaigns. Some figures in business, such as [[Mark Cuban]], have defended companies engaging in "wokeness" by arguing that engaging in social justice causes reflects companies caring about their customers.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2023-06-20 |title=If US companies 'go woke', do they really go broke? |language=en-GB |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-65918155 |access-date=2023-07-20}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Salena Zito: Target and Bud Light are hurting, but Mark Cuban says wokeness is good business |url=https://www.post-gazette.com/opinion/insight/2023/06/11/mark-cuban-bud-light-target-woke/stories/202306110042 |access-date=2023-07-20 |website=Pittsburgh Post-Gazette |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=Go woke, go broke: why Irish Church’s liberal turn should be treated with caution |url=https://thecatholicherald.com/article/go-woke-go-broke-why-irish-churchs-liberal-turn-should-be-treated-with-caution |work=thecatholicherald.com |language=en}}</ref>

When customers boycotted various companies in 2023, such as [[Bud Light]], Target, and The Walt Disney Company, these companies experienced a loss in sales and stock value drops, while Disney lost subscribers to its streaming platform [[Disney+]]. Experts said these losses could not be solely attributed to the boycotts and that they could stem from other causes, such as the companies' responses to the boycotts.<ref name="bbc">{{Cite news |date=2023-06-20 |title=If US companies 'go woke', do they really go broke? |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-65918155 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240222210647/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-65918155 |archive-date=February 22, 2024 |access-date=2023-07-20 |work=BBC News |language=en-GB}}</ref> Some movies said to be "woke" have gone on to financial success, such as [[Greta Gerwig]]'s 2023 movie ''[[Barbie (film)|Barbie]]'', while others, such as ''[[The Marvels]]'', released to a record low box office for Marvel.<ref name=":22">{{Cite web |date=2023-07-26 |title=Conservatives hate woke ''Barbie'', but Greta Gerwig wishes them well |url=https://www.avclub.com/conservatives-hate-woke-barbie-greta-gerwig-wishes-well-1850678001 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230731163747/https://www.avclub.com/conservatives-hate-woke-barbie-greta-gerwig-wishes-well-1850678001 |archive-date=July 31, 2023 |access-date=2023-07-31 |website=[[The A.V. Club]] |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":52">{{Cite web |last=Lowry |first=Brian |date=2023-07-24 |title=''Barbie'' and ''Oppenheimer'' seek one shared goal: Making summer audiences think |url=https://www.cnn.com/2023/07/24/movies/summer-movies-oppenheimer-barbie-analysis/index.html |access-date=2023-07-31 |publisher=[[CNN]] |language=en}}</ref><ref name="forbesMarvels2">{{Cite web |last=Murray |first=Conor |title='The Marvels' Faces Anti-'Woke' Backlash After Box Office Flop—Echoing 'Captain Marvel' Attacks |url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/conormurray/2023/11/13/the-marvels-faces-anti-woke-backlash-after-box-office-flop-echoing-captain-marvel-attacks/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231203191004/https://www.forbes.com/sites/conormurray/2023/11/13/the-marvels-faces-anti-woke-backlash-after-box-office-flop-echoing-captain-marvel-attacks/ |archive-date=December 3, 2023 |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=Forbes |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last=D'Alessandro |first=Anthony |date=May 2, 2024 |title=Disney Detonates Four Bombs In Deadline's 2023 Most Valuable Blockbuster Tournament |url=https://deadline.com/2024/05/biggest-box-office-bombs-2023-lowest-grossing-movies-1235902825/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240503004130/https://deadline.com/2024/05/biggest-box-office-bombs-2023-lowest-grossing-movies-1235902825/ |archive-date=May 3, 2024 |access-date=May 2, 2024 |website=Deadline Hollywood}}</ref> A 2025 study that analyzed responses to 10,000 films concluded that there was no consistent pattern linking progressive themes in movies to harm in the box office results. The study suggested problems may be linked instead to poor execution, such as "heavy-handed political messaging, clumsy canon changes, or anachronistic dialogue".<ref name="FilmStudy2025">{{cite news |last=Pulver |first=Andrew |date=September 27, 2025 |title='Go woke, go broke'? New study challenges claims progressive films flop at the box office |url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2025/sep/27/go-woke-go-broke-new-study-challenges-claims-progressive-films-flop-at-the-box-office |work=The Guardian|archive-url=https://archive.today/20240915092144/https://www.theguardian.com/media/2024/sep/15/go-woke-go-broke-not-true-for-brands-says-global-advertising-study |archive-date=15 September 2024 }}</ref>

=== Validity ===
Some researchers have found little validity in the phrase, arguing instead that diversity and inclusivity may be beneficial.<ref name="inclusion"/><ref name="kantar">{{cite web |title=The woke ad myth is dead – here's why |url=https://www.kantar.com/north-america/Inspiration/Sustainability/The-woke-ad-myth-is-dead-heres-why |access-date=28 September 2025 |website=[[Kantar Group]]}}</ref><ref name="guardianUN2024">{{cite web |last1=Thorpe |first1=Vanessa |last2=Arts |first2=Vanessa Thorpe |date=15 September 2024 |title='Go woke, go broke' not true for brands, says global advertising study |url=https://www.theguardian.com/media/2024/sep/15/go-woke-go-broke-not-true-for-brands-says-global-advertising-study |access-date=28 September 2025 |website=The Guardian|archive-url=https://archive.today/20250927195859/https://www.theguardian.com/film/2025/sep/27/go-woke-go-broke-new-study-challenges-claims-progressive-films-flop-at-the-box-office |archive-date=27 September 2025 }}</ref><ref name="conv2025">{{cite web |last1=Underwood |first1=Peter |date=3 August 2025 |title='Go woke, go broke' is no longer true. Socially aware capitalism is the future of corporate responsibility |url=https://theconversation.com/go-woke-go-broke-is-no-longer-true-socially-aware-capitalism-is-the-future-of-corporate-responsibility-261091 |access-date=28 September 2025 |website=[[The Conversation (website)|The Conversation]]}}</ref>

In 2024, [[Unstereotype Alliance]], a business alliance convened by [[UN Women]] that promotes inclusive advertising, published a study performed by researchers from the [[Saïd Business School]] at the [[University of Oxford]], which analyzed data from 392 brands across 58 countries found that the catchphrase "Go woke, go broke" is wrong. The study found that businesses that authentically and positively portray diverse backgrounds led to a 3.5% increase in shorter-term sales and 16% increase in the longer term.<ref name="inclusion">{{cite web |url=https://www.unstereotypealliance.org/sites/default/files/2024-09/INCLUSIVE%20ADVERTISING%20%28Business%20case%29%20WEB.pdf |title=Inclusion = Income: The Business Case for Inclusive Advertising |work=Unstereotype Alliance }}</ref> The study was regarded as a rebuke to the claim that "going woke" meant companies were "going broke", with reports calling the phrase a "myth" as a result.<ref name="cb2024">{{cite web |date=September 24, 2024 |title=New research busts the myth of go woke and go broke {{!}} Creativebrief |url=https://www.creativebrief.com/bite/thought-leadership/new-research-busts-myth-go-woke-and-go-broke |access-date=28 September 2025 |website=Creativebrief.com |language=en}}</ref><ref name="guardianUN2024" />

In September 2025, film [[industry analyst]] and researcher Stephen Follows published an analysis of responses to 10,000 films, concluding that there was no consistent pattern that would indicate that progressive themes in movies negatively affected the success of movies. Summarizing "So is 'Go Woke, Go Broke' true? The short answer is no", but added "it's not without grains of truth." Follows' analysis found that while some movies with "woke-adjacent theme" may be successful and others fail, "the same is true of films without those themes." His research instead found that the bad outcomes of movies are a result of poor execution, such as "heavy-handed political messaging, clumsy [[Canon (fiction)|canon]] changes, or [[anachronistic]] dialogue reduce audience satisfaction and profitability." His research highlighted that inauthentic changes or portrayal, such as in [[historical film|historical movies]] can increase the risk of failures, while other genres, such as horror, thriller, sports or music films actively benefitted from "diverse casting and [[Cultural identity|identity-driven]] stories" leading to a positive effect on commercial success. Follows' final conclusion was that "'Go Woke, Go Broke' does not hold up as a general rule. Films are too diverse, and audience responses too varied, for a cute slogan to explain outcomes across the whole industry."<ref>{{cite web |last1=Follows |first1=Stephen |title=Is 'Go Woke, Go Broke' true for movies? |url=https://stephenfollows.com/p/is-go-woke-go-broke-true-for-movies |access-date=28 September 2025 |website=stephenfollows.com |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Wiseman |first1=Andreas |date=27 September 2025 |title="Culture & Cinema Are Under Attack", Warns Mk2 Chairman Nathanaël Karmitz During Politics & Entertainment Panel — Zurich Summit |url=https://deadline.com/2025/09/mk2-nathanael-karmitz-culture-and-cinema-are-under-attack-1236557863/ |access-date=28 September 2025 |website=[[Deadline Hollywood|Deadline]]}}</ref> Follows nevertheless noted that there are risk: "Audiences react poorly when identity-driven changes are seen as imposed rather than motivated by character or story. The risks multiply when several shifts occur at once.<ref name="FilmStudy2025" />

=== Early usage ===
The phrase "get woke, go broke" is believed to have been coined by the American military science fiction author [[John Ringo]] in 2018. It originated in a dispute involving Ringo's invitation to [[ConCarolinas]] where objections were raised because Ringo wrote books in which the central character has impulses to rape. The organizers rescinded their invitation, saying that they could not guarantee he would be free of hostile actions from those who objected to his presence at the convention. The incident was reported in a right-wing Web site that has since ceased operation, ''Dangerous.com''; it said that according to Ringo, the convention "pushed its conservative members out of its planning committee, attendance dropped over years, and it's now defunct." Ringo characterized the effect on organizations that give in to the demands of social activists as "Get woke, go broke".<ref>{{cite web |last=Elhefnawy |first=Nader |date=August 21, 2022 |title=Why Does Discussion of Pop Culture Make Up So Large a Part of Political Discourse in Twenty-First Century America? |url=https://ssrn.com/abstract=4196062 |page=26 |doi=10.2139/ssrn.4196062}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |date=April 17, 2018 |title=Author John Ringo Response to SJW assault that led to Sci-Fi convention ban |url=https://www.dangerous.com/43569/author-john-ringo-responds-sjw-assault-led-sci-fi-convention-ban/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190831205807/https://www.dangerous.com/43569/author-john-ringo-responds-sjw-assault-led-sci-fi-convention-ban/ |archive-date=August 31, 2019 |work=Dangerous.com}}</ref> The phrase is then interpreted as a reference to companies that engage in politically correct activities as part of their corporate strategy, but only to see such strategy result in significant financial loss for them.<ref name="stickel">{{cite book |last=Stickel |first=Darryl |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=thBvEAAAQBAJ&pg=PT36 |title=Building Trust: Exceptional Leadership in an Uncertain World |date=2022 |publisher=Forefront Books |isbn=9781637630792 |pages=73–74 |quote=[on go woke, go broke] ... One of the examples often cited is Gillette's 2019 toxic masculinity advertisement}}</ref>

The phrase quickly gained popular usage by those on the right after it was coined. One of the early incidents the phrase was invoked concerned the use of [[Colin Kaepernick]] in [[Nike, Inc.|Nike]]'s "[[Just Do It]]" ad campaign in September 2018, which drew criticism from [[Donald Trump]] and calls for its boycott.<ref>{{cite news |last=Davis |first=Clay |date=September 4, 2018 |title=Why I'm boycotting Nike: Get woke and go broke |url=https://usatoday.com/story/opinion/voices/2018/09/04/colin-kaepernick-ad-boycotting-nike-column/1189989002/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231004083526/https://www.usatoday.com/story/opinion/voices/2018/09/04/colin-kaepernick-ad-boycotting-nike-column/1189989002/ |archive-date=October 4, 2023 |access-date=October 3, 2023 |work=USA Today}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |date=5 September 2018 |title=Nike's Colin Kaepernick ad campaign sends 'terrible message', says Donald Trump |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2018/sep/04/donald-trump-colin-kaepernick-nike-ad-campaign-response |work=The Guardian}}</ref> Nike nevertheless continued with its campaign.<ref>{{cite news |last=Atkinson |first=Claire |date=5 September 2018 |title=Nike doubles down on defiance of Kaepernick criticism, releases full-length TV ad |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/business/business-news/nike-doubles-down-kaepernick-controversy-releases-full-length-tv-ad-n906786 |work=NBC News}}</ref> It also reported increased sales,<ref>{{cite news |date=10 September 2018 |title=Nike sales defy Kaepernick ad campaign backlash |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/business-45472399 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231004093728/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/business-45472399 |archive-date=October 4, 2023 |access-date=October 3, 2023 |work=BBC}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last=Youn |first=Soo |date=December 21, 2018 |title=Nike sales booming after Colin Kaepernick ad, invalidating critics |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Business/nike-sales-booming-kaepernick-ad-invalidating-critics/story?id=59957137 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181222022443/https://abcnews.go.com/Business/nike-sales-booming-kaepernick-ad-invalidating-critics/story?id=59957137 |archive-date=December 22, 2018 |access-date=October 6, 2023 |work=ABC News}}</ref> and this has been used as an example where a company that made a commitment and stayed the course can reap the benefits despite alienating some customers.<ref name="bbc" /> The phrase is also used in political discourse in the [[United Kingdom]].<ref name="BBCRadio">{{cite news |title=Get woke or go broke? |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/programmes/m000dq2w |access-date=14 September 2023 |publisher=BBC |agency=BBC Radio 4}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last1=Ping Chan |first1=Chan |date=1 March 2023 |title=Go woke or go broke, warns CBI |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/business/2023/03/01/go-woke-go-broke-warns-cbi/ |access-date=14 September 2023 |publisher=Daily Telegraph}}</ref>

Another early application of the phrase involved [[Gillette]]. In January 2019, Gillette ran an Internet campaign "[[The Best Men Can Be]]" that criticised [[toxic masculinity]] and suggested how men should act. While the campaign received praise from some quarters, it also received widespread criticism from men who were their core customers, and led to calls for boycott.<ref name="stickel" /><ref>{{cite news |last1=Topping |first1=Alexandra |last2=Lyons |first2=Kate |last3=Weaver |first3=Matthew |date=January 15, 2019 |title=Gillette #MeToo razors ad on 'toxic masculinity' gets praise – and abuse |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/jan/15/gillette-metoo-ad-on-toxic-masculinity-cuts-deep-with-mens-rights-activists |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190117085713/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/jan/15/gillette-metoo-ad-on-toxic-masculinity-cuts-deep-with-mens-rights-activists |archive-date=January 17, 2019 |access-date=September 3, 2023 |work=The Guardian |quote=[On get woke, go broke] ... One of the examples often cited is Gillette's 2019 toxic masculinity advertisement}}</ref> In July 2019, some months after the ad was released, the parent company of Gillette, [[Procter & Gamble]], announced that while it had record sales for other products, it took an $8 billion [[Write-off#Write-down|write-down]] on its revenue for Gillette. The company said that the write-down was due to currency fluctuation, greater competition and a shrinking market as men shave less frequently.<ref>{{cite news |date=July 20, 2019 |title=P&G posts strong sales, takes $8 billion Gillette writedown |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-proctergamble-results-idUSKCN1UP1AD |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230903164339/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-proctergamble-results-idUSKCN1UP1AD |archive-date=September 3, 2023 |access-date=September 3, 2023 |work=Reuters}}</ref> Critics of the campaign, however, believed that the campaign had hurt the company, and this campaign has often been cited as an example of "get woke, go broke".<ref name="stickel" />

Right-wing politicians and individuals used the slogan to refer to the knockout round loss of the [[United States women's national soccer team]] (USWNT) against [[Sweden women's national football team|Sweden]] during the [[2023 FIFA Women's World Cup]], particularly player [[Megan Rapinoe]], who was noted for missing a penalty kick during the shootouts against Sweden's team. The USWNT supports various progressive values, which conservatives argued was the reason they lost.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Abad-Santos |first=Alex |date=2023-08-07 |title=The right-wing backlash against the US women's national soccer team, explained |url=https://www.vox.com/culture/2023/8/7/23823612/us-women-soccer-team-uswnt-world-cup-loss-politics |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230809231514/https://www.vox.com/culture/2023/8/7/23823612/us-women-soccer-team-uswnt-world-cup-loss-politics |archive-date=August 9, 2023 |access-date=2023-08-10 |website=Vox |language=en}}</ref> James Dator of ''[[SB Nation]]'' and Alex Abad-Santos of ''[[Vox (website)|Vox]]'' reported the loss stemming from player injuries, organizational reasons and play, noting Rapinoe did not enter the field until extra time.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Larson |first=Shannon |date=August 7, 2023 |title=Conservatives celebrate the US women's early exit from the World Cup: 'Go woke, go broke' - The Boston Globe |url=https://www.bostonglobe.com/2023/08/07/sports/some-conservatives-delight-us-womens-early-exit-world-cup-go-woke-go-broke/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230809092301/https://www.bostonglobe.com/2023/08/07/sports/some-conservatives-delight-us-womens-early-exit-world-cup-go-woke-go-broke/ |archive-date=August 9, 2023 |access-date=2023-08-10 |website=BostonGlobe.com |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Dator |first=James |date=2023-08-07 |title=USWNT eliminated from World Cup because of their play, not their politics |url=https://www.sbnation.com/soccer/2023/8/7/23822865/uswnt-world-cup-play-not-politics |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230809184843/https://www.sbnation.com/soccer/2023/8/7/23822865/uswnt-world-cup-play-not-politics |archive-date=August 9, 2023 |access-date=2023-08-10 |website=SBNation.com |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":12" />

=== The Walt Disney Company ===
{{Main|Disney and Florida's Parental Rights in Education Act}}

After the passage of Florida's [[Florida Parental Rights in Education Act|Parental Rights in Education Act]], colloquially known as the "Don't Say Gay law" due to its restrictions on "classroom discussion" or giving "classroom instruction"{{efn|The preamble to the act utilizes the phrase "classroom discussion". The relevant numbered section of the act utilizes the phrase "classroom instruction".<ref>{{cite web |title=Florida House Bill 1557 |url=https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2022/1557/BillText/er/PDF |publisher=[[Florida Senate]] |access-date=2022-04-13 |archive-date=2023-02-01 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230201194854/https://www.flsenate.gov/Session/Bill/2022/1557/BillText/er/PDF |url-status=live }}</ref>|name=act}} about [[sexual orientation]] or [[gender identity]] from [[Kindergarten#United States|kindergarten]] through [[Third grade#Examples of the American syllabus|3rd grade]], The Walt Disney Company initially did not take a public stance on the matter. However, as public pressure mounted and in response to employees' concerns, Disney eventually publicly opposed the bill. This stance led to a dispute with Republican Governor [[Ron DeSantis]], who had supported the bill's passage and later expansion to all grades K–12 and has repeatedly criticized Disney for being "woke"<ref>{{Cite news |date=2023-07-29 |title=Florida judge rules against Disney in feud with DeSantis |url=https://www.reuters.com/legal/florida-judge-rules-against-disney-feud-with-desantis-2023-07-28/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230801125158/https://www.reuters.com/legal/florida-judge-rules-against-disney-feud-with-desantis-2023-07-28/ |archive-date=August 1, 2023 |access-date=2023-08-01 |publisher=[[Reuters]] |language=en}}</ref> and accusations of "[[LGBT grooming conspiracy theory|grooming]]".<ref>{{cite news |last=Press-Reynolds |first=Kieran |date=2022-04-15 |title='Grooming' and 'pro-pedophile' surge as conservative buzzwords amid battle over 'Don't Say Gay' bill |url=https://www.insider.com/grooming-pedophile-disney-baseless-buzzword-conservatives-dont-say-gay-2022-4 |access-date=2023-08-01 |work=[[Insider Inc.|Insider]]}}</ref> As a result, the Florida legislature repealed and renamed the Reedy Creek Improvement District, a special taxation ward controlled by Disney and hosting the [[Walt Disney World]] resort, to the [[Central Florida Tourism Oversight District]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-01-06 |title=Florida moves to curtail Disney World's powers as "Don't Say Gay" feud advances |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/dont-say-gay-feud-advances-with-new-proposed-legislation/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230720042342/https://www.cbsnews.com/news/dont-say-gay-feud-advances-with-new-proposed-legislation/ |archive-date=July 20, 2023 |access-date=2023-07-20 |publisher=[[CBS News]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Barnes |first=Brooks |date=2023-05-18 |title=Disney Pulls Plug on $1 Billion Development in Florida |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/05/18/business/disney-ron-desantis-florida.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230720042337/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/05/18/business/disney-ron-desantis-florida.html |archive-date=July 20, 2023 |access-date=2023-07-20 |work=[[The New York Times]] |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Disney responded by [[Disney v. DeSantis|filing a lawsuit]] against Governor DeSantis and cancelling their expansion plans in Florida.

Disney's streaming service [[Disney+]] was reported in February 2023 to have lost over two million subscribers for the last quarter of 2022,<ref>{{Cite web |last=Todd |first=Spangler |date=2023-02-08 |title=Disney+ Drops 2.4 Million Subscribers in First Loss, Bob Iger Heralds 'Significant Transformation' Underway |url=https://variety.com/2023/biz/news/disney-q1-2023-earnings-bob-iger-disney-plus-loses-subscribers-1235517007/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230208215125/https://variety.com/2023/biz/news/disney-q1-2023-earnings-bob-iger-disney-plus-loses-subscribers-1235517007/ |archive-date=February 8, 2023 |access-date=2023-12-28 |website=[[Variety (magazine)|Variety]] |language=en}}</ref> over 4 million the first quarter of 2023,<ref>{{cite news |last=Kollewe |first=Julia |date=May 11, 2023 |title=Disney+ loses 4m subscribers amid exodus in Indian market |url=https://www.theguardian.com/media/2023/may/11/disney-loses-4m-subscribers-amid-exodus-in-indian-market |access-date=2023-12-28 |website=[[The Guardian]] |language=en}}</ref> and over 11 million worldwide the following quarter.<ref name="aug 2023 nyt">{{cite news |last=Barnes |first=Brooks |date=August 9, 2023 |title=For Disney, Streaming Losses and TV's Decline Are a One-Two Punch |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/08/09/business/media/disney-earnings.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231228234314/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/08/09/business/media/disney-earnings.html |archive-date=December 28, 2023 |access-date=2023-12-28 |work=The New York Times}}</ref> Right-wing commentators have attributed the decline to Disney's "woke activism";<ref>{{cite news |date=February 12, 2023 |title='Relentlessly woke activism': Disney Plus loses 2.4 million subscribers |url=https://www.skynews.com.au/opinion/outsiders/relentlessly-woke-activism-disney-plus-loses-24-million-subscribers/video/e07114712f93ec079e9289db4811dc21 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231229001422/https://www.skynews.com.au/opinion/outsiders/relentlessly-woke-activism-disney-plus-loses-24-million-subscribers/video/e07114712f93ec079e9289db4811dc21 |archive-date=December 29, 2023 |access-date=December 28, 2023 |work=Sky News}}</ref> ''[[The New York Times]]'', however, noted that the losses came from India after Disney+ lost the rights to [[Indian Premier League]] cricket matches, while the rest of the world showed an increase of 800,000 subscribers.<ref name="aug 2023 nyt" />

In 2023, a number of films released by Disney under-performed on the box office, including ''[[Ant-Man and the Wasp: Quantumania]]'', ''[[Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny]]'', and ''[[The Marvels]]'', under-performance attributed by some to Disney having gone "woke".<ref name="hassenger">{{cite web |last=Hassenger |first=Jesse |date=November 28, 2023 |title=Fallen kingdom: why has Disney had such a terrible year? |url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2023/nov/28/disney-box-office-wish-the-marvels |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240715204042/https://www.theguardian.com/film/2023/nov/28/disney-box-office-wish-the-marvels |archive-date=July 15, 2024 |access-date=May 21, 2024 |work=The Guardian}}</ref> Others, however, attributed the decline to the expectation that Disney-related films would eventually be released on Disney+, and potential customers chose to wait for the films to be streamed.<ref name="hassenger" /> The 2025 Disney's live-action remake ''[[Snow White (2025 film)|Snow White]]'', widely described by its detractors as "woke",<ref>{{cite news |last=Cartwright |first=Megan |date=March 20, 2025 |title=Is Rachel Zegler's 'Snow White' Woke? Five Major Changes Made to Original |url=https://www.newsweek.com/rachel-zegler-snow-white-woke-changes-original-2047652 |work=Newsweek}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last=Horton |first=Adrian |date=March 21, 2025 |title=How did Snow White become the year's most cursed movie? |url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2025/mar/21/snow-white-disney-controversy |work=The Guardian}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last=Barnes |first=Brooks |date=March 20, 2025 |title=Snow White and the Seven Kajillion Controversies |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2025/03/20/business/snow-white-movie-controversies.html |work=The New York Times}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last=Swain |first=Marianka |date=March 25, 2025 |title=Go woke and go broke – the other Snow White-scale flops |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/2025/03/25/snow-white-ghostbusters-indiana-jones-woke-failures/ |work=The Daily Telegraph}}</ref> under-performed at the box-office resulting in large expected losses for the studio.<ref>{{cite web |last=McClintock |first=Pamela |date=March 24, 2025 |title=Box Office: How 'Snow White' Landed in Potential Bomb Territory |url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/movies/movie-news/is-snow-white-box-office-bomb-1236170816/ |work=Hollywood Reporter}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last=Haigh |first=Joshua |date=March 29, 2025 |title='Woke' Snow White becomes epic flop for Disney |url=https://www.news.com.au/entertainment/movies/woke-snow-white-becomes-epic-flop-for-disney/news-story/d08268560274bd1d3c409751a705665f |work=News.com.au}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last=D'Alessandro |first=Anthony |date=March 31, 2025 |title='Snow White', Poisoned By Controversy At Box Office, Won't Have A Happy Ending With $115M Loss: What Went Wrong |url=https://deadline.com/2025/03/snow-white-bombs-rachel-zegler-1236354912/ |work=Deadline}}</ref> Analysts, however, point to reasons other than 'anti-woke backlash', such as the audience not connecting with the film resulting in mixed reviews, a declining interest in a character from older film, the failure of the studio to counter bad publicity, or that Disney's strategy of live-action adaptation has become "tired".<ref>{{cite news |last=Perry |first=Kevin E G |date=March 25, 2025 |title=Snow White overperforms in red states despite 'Snow Woke' Moniker |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/arts-entertainment/films/news/disney-snow-white-box-office-republicans-b2720918.html |work=The Independent}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last=Faris |first=James |date=April 8, 2025 |title=Disney's 'Snow White' flop is deeper than anti-woke backlash, box office analysts say |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/disney-snow-white-opening-weekend-box-office-woke-backlash-controversy-2025-3 |work=Business Insider}}</ref><ref name="southern">{{cite news |last=Southern |first=Keiran |date=April 3, 2025 |title=As Snow White heads for $115m loss, how can Disney recover? |url=https://www.thetimes.com/us/movie-news/article/snow-white-rachel-zegler-disney-row-ncmm5g6z5 |work=the Times}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last=D'Alessandro |first=Anthony |date=March 23, 2025 |title=Ho-Hum, Ho-Hum: 'Snow White' Opens To $43M — What Poisoned This Princess At The Box Office – Sunday AM Box Office Update |url=https://deadline.com/2025/03/box-office-snow-white-1236346253/ |work=Deadline}}</ref> Nevertheless, film historian Jonathan Kuntz argued that "Disney in the 21st century has been at the forefront of foregrounding race and gender issues and pushing a political agenda ... if you have a political agenda you are going to inevitably alienate half the audience."<ref name="southern" />

=== Bud Light ===
{{Main|2023 Bud Light boycott}}

The beer company [[AB InBev]], which owns [[Bud Light]], partnered with transgender influencer [[Dylan Mulvaney]] on April 1, 2023, sending her custom Bud Light cans featuring her face to celebrate the anniversary of her "365 Days of Girlhood" series, which documented her [[gender transition]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=Martinez |first=Gina |date=April 6, 2023 |title=Bud Light partnership with trans TikTok star Dylan Mulvaney prompts conservative backlash |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/bud-light-dylan-mulvaney-kid-rock-tiktok-backlash-travis-tritt/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230410232815/https://www.cbsnews.com/news/bud-light-dylan-mulvaney-kid-rock-tiktok-backlash-travis-tritt/ |archive-date=April 10, 2023 |access-date=July 20, 2023 |work=CBS}}</ref><ref name=":02">{{Cite web |last=Stewart |first=Emily |date=2023-04-12 |title=The Bud Light boycott?? because sure, explained |url=https://www.vox.com/money/2023/4/12/23680135/bud-light-boycott-dylan-mulvaney-travis-tritt-trans |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230426130119/https://www.vox.com/money/2023/4/12/23680135/bud-light-boycott-dylan-mulvaney-travis-tritt-trans |archive-date=April 26, 2023 |access-date=2023-04-26 |website=Vox |language=en}}</ref> In response, several notable figures on the American right called for a boycott of the company.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Ulea |first=Anca |date=2023-04-10 |title=Why are conservatives lashing out at Nike and Bud Light? |url=https://www.euronews.com/culture/2023/04/10/bud-light-and-nike-face-conservative-backlash-over-partnerships-with-trans-influencer-dyla |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230416230637/https://www.euronews.com/culture/2023/04/10/bud-light-and-nike-face-conservative-backlash-over-partnerships-with-trans-influencer-dyla |archive-date=April 16, 2023 |access-date=2023-04-13 |website=[[euronews]] |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":02" /> In the month following the advertisement, Bud Light sales dropped.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Valinsky |first=Jordan |date=2023-04-26 |title=Bud Light sales are falling, but distributors say they're sticking by the brand |url=https://www.cnn.com/2023/04/26/business/bud-light-distributors-response/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230508182317/https://www.cnn.com/2023/04/26/business/bud-light-distributors-response/index.html |archive-date=May 8, 2023 |access-date=2023-05-07 |publisher=[[CNN Business]] |language=en}}</ref> During a conference call with investors on May 4, 2023, AB InBev's CEO [[Michel Doukeris]] said the drop in Bud Light sales "would represent around 1% of our overall global volumes for that period."<ref>{{Cite web |last=Mayer |first=Grace |title=What boycott? Bud Light controversy led to only a 1% drop in global sales volume, CEO says |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/anheuser-busch-ceo-response-bud-light-dylan-mulvaney-can-sales-2023-5 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230510112531/https://www.businessinsider.com/anheuser-busch-ceo-response-bud-light-dylan-mulvaney-can-sales-2023-5 |archive-date=May 10, 2023 |access-date=2023-05-07 |website=[[Business Insider]] |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=2023-05-04 |title=Anheuser-Busch InBev/NV (BUD) Q1 2023 Earnings Call Transcript |url=https://www.fool.com/earnings/call-transcripts/2023/05/04/anheuser-busch-inbevnv-bud-q1-2023-earnings-call-t/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230512124404/https://www.fool.com/earnings/call-transcripts/2023/05/04/anheuser-busch-inbevnv-bud-q1-2023-earnings-call-t/ |archive-date=May 12, 2023 |access-date=2023-05-07 |work=[[The Motley Fool]] Transcribing |language=en}}</ref> The company placed its marketing vice president, who authorized the promotion, Alissa Heinerscheid, on administrative leave.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Hur |first=Krystal |date=2023-04-24 |title=''Wall Street Journal'': Bud Light owner places two execs on leave after transgender influencer backlash |url=https://www.cnn.com/2023/04/24/business/anheuser-execs-on-leave/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230801125158/https://www.cnn.com/2023/04/24/business/anheuser-execs-on-leave/index.html |archive-date=August 1, 2023 |access-date=2023-08-01 |publisher=[[CNN Business]] |language=en}}</ref>

Bernd Debusmann Jr, writing for [[BBC News]], reported that experts believed the boycott's success depended on the company's response, and they considered Bud Light to have made a mistake when handling the boycott. Public relations professor Tony D'Angelo considered the company's stance to be "waffling", stating "If you waffle, then people are going to rightly question what they really stand for".<ref name="bbc" /> While research suggests that consumer boycotts tended to be short-lived,<ref>{{cite web |last=Sullivan |first=Becky |date=April 27, 2023 |title=Bud Light sales dip after trans promotion, but such boycotts are often short-lived |url=https://www.npr.org/2023/04/27/1172299478/bud-light-sales-fall-trans-influencer-boycott |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230828075051/https://www.npr.org/2023/04/27/1172299478/bud-light-sales-fall-trans-influencer-boycott |archive-date=August 28, 2023 |access-date=October 4, 2023 |website=[[NPR]]}}</ref> the effect of this boycott continued for some months, and Bud Light lost its leading position as America's best-selling beer, with the company's profits in the US dropping by more than 28% in the quarter.<ref>{{cite news |date=August 3, 2023 |title=Bud Light boycott over trans influencer Dylan Mulvaney hits beer giant's sales |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/business-66398296 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231005171554/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/business-66398296 |archive-date=October 5, 2023 |access-date=October 4, 2023 |work=BBC}}</ref> Owen Myers of the ''Guardian'' described the boycott as one of the biggest in US history.<ref>{{cite news |last=Myers |first=Owen |title='Panic and rash decision-making': ex-Bud Light staff on one of the biggest boycotts in US history |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2023/sep/19/dylan-mulvaney-bud-light-boycott |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240715204055/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2023/sep/19/dylan-mulvaney-bud-light-boycott |archive-date=July 15, 2024 |access-date=October 4, 2023 |work=The Guardian}}</ref>

=== Cinema ===
A study by Film data analyst Stephen Follows in 2025 analyzed responses to over 10,000 films concluded that there was no consistent pattern linking progressive themes in movies to their box office results. The study instead said that "Problems … are linked to execution," The study noted that for some genres such as horror, sports and music, diversity and identity narratives in films can have "substantially positive" effect on the box-office. However, for big-budget franchises, a film perceived to have deviated from its "core canon" due to execution issues may increase financial risks, saying "Heavy-handed political messaging, clumsy canon changes, or anachronistic dialogue reduce audience satisfaction and profitability."<ref name="FilmStudy2025" />

The phrase was notably used in the context of the [[Marvel Cinematic Universe]] films ''[[Captain Marvel (film)|Captain Marvel]]'' and ''[[Black Panther: Wakanda Forever]]'', which challenged the notion of diversity backlash and the success of boycotts against the films.<ref name=":6">{{Cite news |last1=Lacina |first1=Bethany |last2=Goren |first2=Lilly J. |date=2022-11-17 |title=Analysis {{!}} Is 'Wakanda Forever' too 'woke' for Marvel's own good? |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2022/11/11/black-panther-wakanda-mcu-woke/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230329104927/https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2022/11/11/black-panther-wakanda-mcu-woke/ |archive-date=March 29, 2023 |access-date=2023-08-07 |newspaper=Washington Post |language=en-US |issn=0190-8286}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2019-03-15 |title=Analysis {{!}} The smash success of 'Captain Marvel' shows us that conservatives are ignoring the alt-right |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2019/03/15/captain-marvels-smash-success-shows-us-that-conservatives-are-ignoring-alt-right/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190316161008/https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2019/03/15/captain-marvels-smash-success-shows-us-that-conservatives-are-ignoring-alt-right/ |archive-date=March 16, 2019 |access-date=2023-08-07 |newspaper=Washington Post |language=en-US |issn=0190-8286}}</ref> Regarding ''Captain Marvel'', Bethany Lacina, writing for ''[[The Washington Post]]'', concluded that there likely was no strong conservative resistance when she analyzed search traffic and Fox News coverage of the film.
